pageDescription: See how I planned to execute this project.
content: [{"id":"d0edf1f4-e5e4-428f-9c2b-6b43a7eb6757","component":"Text","data":{"content":"My initial approach was to use Roslyn. Roslyn allows developers to extend the C# compiler through analyzers and source generators, so my first idea was to implement InlineXML as a Roslyn extension. The goal was to extend the language in a way that would allow JSX-like syntax to be embedded directly within C#.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"040dc052-a219-43e6-b0a0-1418e7cf98ef","component":"Text","data":{"content":"However, after experimenting with this approach, I realized that Roslyn does not allow modifications to the core language syntax in this way. Because of that limitation, the original idea had to be abandoned. Instead, InlineXML would need to be implemented as a preprocessing step that transforms the InlineXML syntax into valid C# before compilation.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"2e075261-14fb-4431-8d4b-809d362475fc","component":"Text","data":{"content":"One of the main challenges when implementing JSX-like syntax in C# was ensuring that it would not be confused with generics or other language constructs. A pattern I frequently use with JSX components is returning JSX expressions wrapped in parentheses, for example return (<Component />);. This pattern turned out to be particularly useful for parsing.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"e28ce37c-8ba2-4522-ab3a-72ebe66964b8","component":"Text","data":{"content":"In C#, the < character is not a valid standalone operator in this context. This allows the parser to reliably detect expressions where the first child node is a less-than symbol, distinguishing JSX from generic type declarations or comparison operators. InlineXML therefore requires that JSX expressions at the outermost level be wrapped in parentheses. While this introduces a minor restriction, it improves code readability and makes the parsing logic more deterministic.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"9cb56397-564a-4bcf-baab-8852abf2966a","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The parentheses requirement applies only to the surrounding node. Once the outer expression is wrapped, nested JSX expressions are processed recursively using the same rules. This recursive approach allows deeply nested JSX structures to be written naturally while maintaining correct parsing and avoiding ambiguity with C# syntax. By combining the parentheses restriction with recursive matching, InlineXML maximizes flexibility for component composition while ensuring clarity and correctness throughout the component tree.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"5334d4bf-f746-41f6-a813-7612fca8a680","component":"Text","data":{"content":"To preserve the developer experience, this transformation needed to happen transparently. I implemented a separate binary that watches the project files for changes. When a file containing InlineXML is modified, the tool automatically regenerates the corresponding C# code, ensuring the transformed output stays in sync with the source without requiring any manual steps from the developer.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"189b3243-ba07-4b80-975d-a7f8775277c3","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The VS Code extension also handled automatic setup. When installed, the plugin would download the InlineXML transformation binary, so the language server could run immediately without any manual configuration. This made it easy for developers to start editing .csx files, with transformation, diagnostics, and source mapping working automatically.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"56662578-a274-432f-ac51-0e43fdc46674","component":"Text","data":{"content":"With the JSX-like conventions, where lowercase tag names represent HTML elements and uppercase tag names represent components, the extension provided a fully integrated development experience within VS Code while staying fully compatible with the .NET ecosystem.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"93a22ba9-18e4-4a50-9f55-8c0b804ef2e1","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The reason I added HTML tag support was that it provided immediate value. With JSX-like syntax, developers could write standard HTML elements directly in InlineXML and have them rendered in the browser. This allowed the same syntax used for components to also generate real HTML, making it possible to serve pages directly using InlineXML without needing additional templating or rendering logic.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]
